title: Southwest Airlines & Final Exam Review
keyQuestion: How do we integrate operations strategy with all course concepts for the final exam?
overview: This comprehensive review session covers Southwest Airlines' operations strategy and integrates all major course concepts for the final exam. Southwest exemplifies perfect alignment between operations and marketing strategy through interlocking tactics like point-to-point service, fast turnarounds, and standardized aircraft. We'll also work through quantitative problems in queueing, EOQ, and newsvendor while reviewing key principles from capacity analysis, factory physics, and supply chain management.

coreContent.title: Southwest Airlines Operations Strategy (CQDF Analysis)
coreContent.content:
For Southwest's traditional model, analyze their operations objectives:

Objective | Define | Rank | Measure
Cost | Lowest cost | 1 | Cost per passenger; cost per flight; fuel costs; staffing costs
Quality | Standard / low | 3 | Clean planes; no meals; no frills; fun
Delivery | Frequent flights; on time; we get you there; ease to change flights | 2 | On time percent; flights per day between cities; price for a customer to change flights
Flexibility | Low flexibility; no first class; no meals or frills | 4 | Number of options for flight services and seats

Interlocking Ops Strategy/Tactics: Point-to-point Service, Fast Turn Time, Low Cost Operations, High Demand, Smaller Airports, Boarding Process, Ticketing Process, Slow Growth, All 737 Aircraft, Low Prices, No Meals, SWA Culture
coreContent.keyInsight: Alignment of operations and marketing strategy is critical to a successful business strategy. The best operations strategies are supported through many individual elements (levers/tactics) that are consistent with the objectives (C,Q,D,F)

coreContent.title: Southwest's Operational Efficiency Analysis
coreContent.content:
A Simple Quick-Turn Analysis:
Assume | Minutes
 | SW | Industry Ave
Air Time | 115 | 115
Turn Time | 25 | 60
Total | 140 | 175
Percent Increase | 25% |

Conclusion: Competitors need 25% more planes
Planes: Southwest 539 Boeing 737s vs Competitor 674 Boeing 737s
Cost of planes: $8,085,000,000 vs $10,110,000,000 ($60M/plane)
Annual cost: $808,500,000 (10% cost of capital)

A Simple Meal Cost Analysis:
Meals: Cost per meal $10.00
Seats: 137, Load factor: 0.712, Full seats: 97.5
Meal cost per flight: $975
Flights per day: 3300, Percent with meals: 0.333
Meal cost per day: $1,071,911
Annual: $391,247,521
coreContent.keyInsight: While each element may have value, competitors cannot achieve parity by simply copying individual elements – the whole is more than the sum of the parts

coreContent.title: Final Exam Review: Queueing Problems
coreContent.content:
Better Buzz Coffee Problem:
Two students measured service times: average 3.3 minutes, standard deviation 4.8 minutes
Two other students measured time between arrivals: average 4.0 minutes, standard deviation 3.2 minutes
Only one server working during this time

Based on this information:
1. What would be the average time in line?
2. What would be the average number of customers in line?
3. What would the average waiting time in line be if Better Buzz added a second server?

Solution approach:
ρ = M/A = 3.3/4.0 = 0.825
Ca = σa/A = 3.2/4.0 = 0.8, Ca² = 0.64
Cs = σs/M = 4.8/3.3 = 1.45, Cs² = 2.11
W = M × [ρ/(1-ρ)] × [(Ca² + Cs²)/2]
W = 3.3 × [0.825/(1-0.825)] × [(0.64 + 2.11)/2]
W = 3.3 × 4.71 × 1.375 = 21.4 minutes
coreContent.keyInsight: Waiting time increases dramatically as utilization gets very high. Variability in either arrivals or service times magnifies the effects of utilization on waiting times

coreContent.title: Final Exam Review: EOQ Problems
coreContent.content:
Shovel Retailer Problem:
A large retailer pays $45.20 per unit, fixed cost for placing an order is $19.50, capital cost is 17%, physical holding costs negligible, annual demand is 22,500 units

1. Determine the optimal order quantity: EOQ = √(2SD/H) = √(2×19.50×22,500/(45.20×0.17)) = √(877,500/7.684) = √114,205 = 338 units
2. Total annual relevant cost: TC = S(D/Q) + H(Q/2) = 19.50×(22,500/338) + 7.684×(338/2) = $1,298 + $1,296 = $2,594
3. Current order quantity is 500 units. Savings = TC(500) - TC(338) = $2,635 - $2,594 = $41
4. Merger impact: Combined demand = 45,000 units. New EOQ = √(2×19.50×45,000/7.684) = 478 units. Combined TC = $3,670. Separate TC = 2×$2,594 = $5,188. Savings = 29.2%
coreContent.keyInsight: EOQ scales by √n for n locations consolidated, creating square-root savings. Consolidating demand creates efficiency gains

coreContent.title: Final Exam Review: Newsvendor Problems
coreContent.content:
Soccer World Cup Book Problem:
Bookstore must order in advance. Mean demand = 180 units, standard deviation = 65. Buy for $23, sell for $50, discount price = $10

1. How many should they buy?
Co = Cost - Salvage = 23 - 10 = $13
Cu = Price - Cost = 50 - 23 = $27
pc = Cu/(Cu + Co) = 27/(27 + 13) = 0.675
Q* = NORM.INV(0.675, 180, 65) = 210 units

2. Probability of running out = 1 - service level = 1 - 0.675 = 32.5%

3. For Q = 190: Service level = NORM.DIST(190, 180, 65, TRUE) = 56%

4. Service Level Curve: As you approach 100% service, it becomes increasingly difficult to increase service further due to the shape of the normal distribution. The curve is convex - steeper at high service levels
coreContent.keyInsight: As service level approaches 100%, order quantity increases exponentially. This explains why 100% service is rarely economical

coreContent.title: Supply Chain Management Review
coreContent.content:
Bullwhip Effect (Functional/Replenishment Products like eBeer):
Problem: Information distortion and order variability grows as you move up supply chain
Causes: Over-reaction to backlogs, neglecting inventory position, lack of communication/coordination, delay times
Solutions: Share information internally (S&OP), externally (CPFR), share decision rights (VMI)

Innovative/Style Products (Sport Obermeyer):
Challenges: Long lead times, competitive/trend uncertainty, short product life cycles, global production
Strategies: Use information to quantify risk, forecast distributions not just means, use newsvendor model, reduce risk with early order information, use flexibility/redundancy, increase reactive capacity, hedge with common components and pre-positioned inventories
coreContent.keyInsight: Different product/supply characteristics require different supply chain strategies. Functional products need efficient supply chains; innovative products need responsive supply chains

coreContent.title: Supply Chain Risk Management Framework
coreContent.content:
Managing Supply Chain Risks:
Anticipatory Strategies (buy options): Information & Planning (scenario planning, real-time risk monitoring), Supply Chain Process Adjustments (strategic inventory, directed buy agreements), Supply Chain Structure Design (footprint restructuring, multi-region sourcing, strategic partnerships, vertical integration)

Reactive Adaptations (exercise options): Shifts in production/procurement volumes, collaborative adaptation of sources/logistics/technologies, priority allocations

Specific Strategies: Use inventory buffers (safety stock), time buffers (safety time), information sharing (S&OP, CPFR, VMI), flexibility/redundancy (reactive capacity), currency hedging
coreContent.keyInsight: Two-stage approach: anticipatory strategies create options for later use, reactive adaptations exercise those options when needed

coreContent.title: Operations Principles Summary
coreContent.content:
Three things that hurt you in operations:
1. Setups - capacity may depend on order size, find ways to speed up setup times, set up offline, specialized setup personnel
2. Bottlenecks - system performance is determined by limiting resource, bottlenecks may move as order size changes, doubling bottleneck capacity may not double system capacity
3. Uncertainty & Variability - variability decreases capacity, creates need for WIP management to balance throughput time vs capacity

...but proper actions mitigate this pain:
- Tools: Capacity Analysis, Setup Reduction, Little's Law, WIP management, Queueing formulas, EOQ model, Newsvendor model
- Insights: Focused operations exploit power of rigid processes, rebalancing resources extracts value, managing customer perceptions matters
coreContent.keyInsight: The three pain points appear throughout all operations contexts - process analysis, factory physics, queueing, inventory management, and supply chain design

variables.Ca: Coefficient of variation of arrivals = σa/A
variables.Cs: Coefficient of variation of service = σs/M

Sum of demands from all locations

formulas.name: Little's Law

variables.WIP: Work in process (average number in system)

formulas.workedExample: Grohe Lux: Before WIP=7500, TPT=4.3, Rate=1744. After WIP=1200, TPT=1, Rate=1200

practiceProblems.question: Analyze Southwest's CQDF priorities and explain how they align with operational tactics.
practiceProblems.solution: Cost #1 (lowest cost carrier), Delivery #2 (frequent flights, on-time), Quality #3 (standard/no frills), Flexibility #4 (limited options). Tactics: point-to-point service, fast turns, all 737s, no meals, smaller airports - all support cost leadership while maintaining acceptable delivery.
practiceProblems.source: Southwest After Class Handouts + Final Exam Review

keyInsights: Three things that hurt you in operations: Setups, Bottlenecks, Uncertainty & Variability ...but proper actions mitigate this pain
Alignment of operations and marketing strategy is critical to a successful business strategy
The best operations strategies are supported through many individual elements that are consistent with objectives
Competitors cannot achieve parity by copying individual elements – the whole is more than the sum of the parts
Waiting time increases dramatically as utilization gets very high
Variability in arrivals or service times magnifies utilization effects on waiting
EOQ scales by √n for consolidated operations, creating square-root savings
As service level approaches 100%, order quantity increases exponentially
Different product characteristics require different supply chain strategies
Functional products need efficient supply chains; innovative products need responsive supply chains
Information sharing reduces bullwhip effect in supply chains
Supply chain risk management requires both anticipatory strategies and reactive adaptations
